title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read four extracts from internet articles about technology in sports. For questions 37 – 40, choose from the experts A – D. The expert may be chosen more than once.

content.title: Less is More: The Four-Day Week

texts.A: The five-day workweek is a social construct that no longer serves the needs of a 24/7 digital economy. First codified during the Industrial Revolution as a hard-won labour reform, this structure has since calcified into an unquestioned norm that actively stifles both creativity and employee wellbeing in the modern era. [37]Trials have shown that a reduction in hours leads to a surge in productivity, as employees are more focused and less prone to the 'presenteeism' that plagues modern offices.[/37] [39]Furthermore, the carbon savings from reduced commuting and office energy use are a significant fringe benefit.[/39] We must stop measuring value by 'time spent' and start measuring it by 'results achieved.' Countries such as Iceland and New Zealand have already demonstrated that this transition is not only viable but economically beneficial. It is time to treat workers as adults who can manage their own schedules rather than subordinates requiring constant supervision.
texts.B: While the idea of a three-day weekend is enticing, the economic reality for small businesses is far from rosy. The enthusiasts of the four-day week tend to work in technology or media—sectors where output can be shifted and meetings rescheduled—rather than in hospitality, logistics, or healthcare, where service delivery is inherently time-dependent and cannot simply be compressed. [38]I disagree with the notion that productivity increases will magically cover the cost of a 20% reduction in labor time; in service industries, if the shop is closed, no revenue is generated.[/38] [40]Moreover, for many workers, a four-day week simply means cramming 40 hours of stress into four days, leading to higher rates of burnout, not lower.[/40] We risk creating a two-tier workforce where only the 'cushy' white-collar jobs enjoy the benefits of flexibility. Any serious policy reform must grapple with this uneven distribution; otherwise, the burden of five-day labour will simply fall on those already most economically vulnerable.
texts.C: The four-day week is the only logical response to the mental health crisis currently engulfing the global workforce. Burnout has been formally recognized by the World Health Organization as an occupational phenomenon, and the financial cost of employee disengagement and absenteeism to global GDP runs into the trillions annually. [37]I share the view that giving people more time for rest and family leads to a more loyal and energized staff, which ultimately benefits the bottom line.[/37] [38]However, I am less convinced than some that this is a 'one-size-fits-all' solution.[/38] For it to work, it requires a complete rethink of management structures, moving away from micromanagement and toward genuine autonomy. Without this fundamental cultural shift, the shorter week is just a gimmick that risks increasing workload intensity without improving the quality of employees' lives. The quality of time matters as much as its quantity. Lasting change requires not just a different timetable but a fundamentally different relationship between employers and the people they employ, built on trust, transparency, and shared accountability for outcomes.
texts.D: The focus on 'productivity' in the four-day week debate is a red herring. As artificial intelligence and automation absorb increasingly large portions of the routine knowledge economy, the real policy challenge is ensuring that the gains from these technologies are distributed equitably rather than captured entirely by shareholders. [40]I share the view that a shorter week is an essential tool for preventing burnout and ensuring that the benefits of technology are shared by all, not just a few shareholders.[/40] [39]I am skeptical, however, about the 'green' claims; if people use their extra day off to fly for a mini-break or go on long drives, the environmental impact could actually be negative.[/39] We need to ensure that more free time leads to more sustainable lifestyles, not more consumption. A shorter working week, disconnected from broader social and environmental policy, is unlikely to deliver the transformative societal change its most ardent advocates routinely promise.

questions.question: Which expert has a similar view to A on the impact of a shorter workweek on employee performance?
questions.explanation: A mentions a 'surge in productivity' and more focus. C shares this, stating that more rest leads to a 'more loyal and energized staff' which 'benefits the bottom line.'

questions.question: Which expert expresses a different opinion to A regarding the universal applicability of the four-day week?
questions.explanation: A presents the 4-day week as a necessary shift for the 'digital economy' (broadly positive). C, while agreeing it's good, is 'less convinced than some that this is a one-size-fits-all solution,' noting it requires specific management changes.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from A on the environmental benefits of the four-day week?
questions.explanation: A highlights 'carbon savings' as a significant benefit. D is 'skeptical' about these green claims, arguing that more free time might lead to 'negative' environmental impacts if people travel more.

questions.question: Which expert shares B's concern about the potential for increased stress in a four-day week?
questions.explanation: B fears that cramming hours into fewer days will lead to 'higher rates of burnout.' D also identifies the shorter week as a tool that *should* prevent burnout, but notes that the current focus is wrong, implying the same link between work-structure and mental health (though D is more optimistic about the goal, both B and D connect the 4-day week to the burnout debate).
